snj.title: The Service National de la Jeunesse – Empowering Young People in Luxembourg
snj.description: What the SNJ (National Youth Service) is, its motto 'Jonker staark maachen', the youth sector it supports, its hands-on activity centres, the help it gives young people moving into working life (including the voluntary service), and how it listens to young people and promotes safe use of technology through BEE SECURE.

questions.text: What does the SNJ do?
options: It is Luxembourg's National Youth Service, supporting young people's development
questions.explanation: The Service National de la Jeunesse (SNJ) is Luxembourg's public National Youth Service, created in 1964. Its motto is “Jonker staark maachen” — empowering youth. It helps young people develop and become responsible, active citizens.

questions.text: What kind of education is the SNJ's work based on?
options: Non-formal education — learning through activities and experiences
questions.explanation: The SNJ works through non-formal education: learning outside the classroom, through activities, projects and real experiences. Participation is voluntary.

questions.text: What is the voluntary service offered by the SNJ?
options: A chance for a young person to commit to a project of general interest and gain experience
questions.explanation: The voluntary service lets a young person take part in a real project of general interest for a period of time. It is learning by doing — a stepping stone towards training, a job, or finding direction.

questions.text: Who can the SNJ help with the transition to working life?
options: Young people at risk of dropping out, early school leavers and inactive young people
questions.explanation: The SNJ supports young people who are at risk of dropping out, who left school early, or who have been inactive, helping each build a personal “life project” through workshops, training and practical alternatives.

questions.text: What is BEE SECURE?
options: An SNJ initiative promoting safe, responsible use of the internet and technology
questions.explanation: BEE SECURE promotes the safe and responsible use of the internet and new technologies, with practical advice for young people and parents to avoid online risks.

enfance.title: The Office National de l'Enfance – Support and Protection for Children and Families
enfance.description: What the Office National de l'Enfance (ONE) is, how it works with families voluntarily and step by step to protect children's wellbeing, its fourteen regional offices, the professionals and help measures it offers (from practical and psychological support to foster care), and how to ask for help.

questions.text: What is the main aim of the Office National de l'Enfance?
options: To protect children's wellbeing and support children, young people and families
questions.explanation: The ONE's purpose is to protect the wellbeing of the child and to support children, young people and families going through difficult times, working voluntarily and collaboratively with them.

questions.text: How does the ONE work with families?
options: It takes decisions without the family
It works with families voluntarily, step by step, with respect
questions.explanation: The ONE aims for voluntary collaboration. Its professionals build help measures together with the family, around the family's real needs, and keep evaluating what works.

questions.text: How many regional offices does the ONE have across Luxembourg?
options: Fourteen regional childhood offices across the country
questions.explanation: The ONE has fourteen regional childhood offices spread across Luxembourg, which families visit by appointment, so support is available near where people live.

questions.text: Which professionals work at the ONE to support families?
options: Psychologists, social workers and trained educators
questions.explanation: The ONE's teams include psychologists, social workers and trained educators. They meet families, visit homes, set up help measures with the family, and coordinate with other services.

questions.text: How can a family start asking the ONE for help?
options: Using the request-for-help form on the ONE's website, or via a professional
It is not possible to ask directly
questions.explanation: A family can contact the ONE directly using the request-for-help form on its website; the nearest regional office then gets in touch. A professional working with the family can also help start the request.

cepas.title: CePAS – Psychological and School Support for Young People
cepas.description: What CePAS (the psycho-social and school support centre) is, its sixty-year history, the consultation centre for young people aged 12–30 and their families, the quick support available inside every secondary school, consultations now also in English, and how it connects with other services.

questions.text: What is CePAS?
options: A psycho-social and school support centre for young people
questions.explanation: CePAS, the Centre psycho-social et d'accompagnement scolaires, supports the personal, relational and academic development and wellbeing of young people at school. It was founded in 1965 and marked sixty years in 2025.

questions.text: Who can use CePAS's consultation centre?
options: Adolescents and young adults aged about 12 to 30, and their families
questions.explanation: The consultation centre is for adolescents and young adults aged roughly 12 to 30, and their parents and families, offering psychological and therapeutic support for school, personal or relationship problems.

questions.text: Where can a young person get quick support, often without an appointment?
options: At the psycho-social service present in every secondary school
questions.explanation: A psycho-social service is present in every secondary school (lycée), where a young person can often be seen quickly, sometimes the same day, without an appointment.

questions.text: What changed at CePAS in early 2026?
options: Consultations became available in English as well
questions.explanation: Since early 2026, consultations at CePAS's centre for young people and families are also available in English, opening the service to many more families in Luxembourg's international community.

questions.text: How does CePAS work with the rest of the support system?
options: Together with parents, schools and services like the Office National de l'Enfance
questions.explanation: CePAS works hand in hand with parents, teachers and schools, and is one of the partners the Office National de l'Enfance can call on for psychological support — so help can be coordinated around the young person.

fondseuropeens.title: European Funds in Luxembourg – How EU Money Supports Local Projects
fondseuropeens.description: What the European Funds are, how the EU's cohesion policy invests in member countries, the main funds (regional development, the social fund, and asylum & migration), how managing authorities oversee them, what they pay for, and how organisations can take part.

questions.text: What is the idea behind the EU's cohesion policy?
options: To collect money from member countries
To invest in regions and people so they can develop fairly across Europe
questions.explanation: The EU's cohesion policy invests in regions and people to help them develop fairly across Europe. Each member country, including Luxembourg, receives a share to spend on agreed goals.

questions.text: What does the European Social Fund Plus (FSE+) mainly support?
options: People — jobs, skills, training and social inclusion
questions.explanation: The European Social Fund Plus (FSE+) is about people: employment, skills and training, and including people who are left behind, helping to fight poverty and exclusion.

questions.text: What is a “managing authority”?
options: The official body responsible for running a fund's programme properly
questions.explanation: For each fund there is a managing authority — the official body that selects projects, manages the money carefully and checks results, guided by the principle of sound financial management.

questions.text: What does “co-financing” mean for these projects?
options: The EU pays for everything alone
The EU funds join with national money to make a project possible
Citizens must pay back the money
questions.explanation: European funds usually co-finance projects — they join forces with national money rather than paying for everything. Projects are often run by local associations, companies or public bodies.

questions.text: Who can apply to use European funds in Luxembourg?
options: Organisations and projects that fit the goals — associations, companies, public bodies
questions.explanation: Organisations running projects that fit the funds' goals can find out how to take part on the European Funds website, which also explains the objectives and shows examples of funded projects.

zesumme.title: Zesumme Vereinfachen – Helping to Simplify Public Services Together
zesumme.description: What the 'Zesumme Vereinfachen' platform is, how citizens and businesses can submit ideas to simplify the State's procedures, who runs it (the Ministry for Digitalisation), how you can take part in one or several phases in any of the four languages, and a real example: improving the MyGuichet app.
topics: Run by the Ministry for Digitalisation

questions.text: What is “Zesumme Vereinfachen”?
options: A platform where citizens and businesses help simplify the State's procedures
questions.explanation: “Zesumme Vereinfachen” (meaning “simplifying together”) is an online platform where citizens and businesses can submit ideas and help make the State's administrative procedures simpler.

questions.text: Who runs the Zesumme Vereinfachen platform?
options: The Ministry for Digitalisation, through the Simplification Council
questions.explanation: The platform is run by the Ministry for Digitalisation through the Simplification Council. It launched in 2022, as part of the wider “Einfach Lëtzebuerg” simplification programme.

questions.text: How much do you have to commit to take part?
options: You must follow every project from start to finish
You can take part in just one phase, or several, as you wish
You must be an elected official
questions.explanation: Participants are free: you can get involved in just one project phase or in several, as much as you want. You are not committed to follow a project from start to finish.

questions.text: In which languages can you contribute?
options: None — only in writing by post
questions.explanation: The platform welcomes contributions in all four languages — Luxembourgish, German, French and English — so everyone in multilingual Luxembourg can take part in their own language.

questions.text: What is one real example of the platform's impact?
options: Citizen feedback helped improve the MyGuichet app
questions.explanation: Citizen feedback through the platform has helped improve the MyGuichet app — the State's online counter — showing how one person's suggestion can improve a service used for millions of procedures.
